content.text: Resources vs Tools — The Core Distinction
MCP exposes two primary capability types to clients, and the distinction matters for both design and client behaviour:
Data the LLM can read (files, DB rows, API snapshots)
Client or user explicitly pulls it
An operation the LLM can invoke
Possible — tools may write, send, or execute
side of an integration (a file, a document, a config value). Tools model the
side (search, create, send, execute). A well-designed MCP server exposes both: resources for data the LLM needs to read, tools for operations the LLM needs to perform.

If a capability has no side effects and just returns data, make it a resource. If it executes something, writes data, or calls an external API, make it a tool. The distinction matters because clients can apply different access control and confirmation policies to each.
A resource has a URI, a name, an optional description, and a MIME type. The URI scheme is arbitrary — you define your own conventions. Resources can be listed (

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.types import Resource, TextContent, BlobResourceContents
import base64, os

app = Server("document-server")

@app.list_resources()
async def list_resources():
    return [
        Resource(
            uri="docs://readme",
            name="README",
            description="Project README file.",
            mimeType="text/markdown",
        ),
        Resource(
            uri="docs://changelog",
            name="CHANGELOG",
            description="Recent changes and version history.",
            mimeType="text/markdown",
        ),
        Resource(
            uri="config://app-settings",
            name="App Settings",
            description="Current application configuration (read-only).",
            mimeType="application/json",
        ),
    ]

@app.read_resource()
async def read_resource(uri: str):
    if uri == "docs://readme":
        with open("README.md") as f:
            return [TextContent(type="text", text=f.read())]
    if uri == "docs://changelog":
        with open("CHANGELOG.md") as f:
            return [TextContent(type="text", text=f.read())]
    if uri == "config://app-settings":
        import json
        config = {"version": "2.1.0", "debug": False, "max_results": 50}
        return [TextContent(type="text", text=json.dumps(config, indent=2))]
    return [TextContent(type="text", text=f"Unknown resource: {uri}")]
Resources can be dynamic: the URI includes variables (
) resolved at read time. Declare them in

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.types import Resource, TextContent
import re

app = Server("order-server")

@app.list_resources()
async def list_resources():
    # URI template signals to the client that this is parameterised
    return [
        Resource(
            uri="orders://{order_id}",
            name="Order Record",
            description="Retrieve a single order by ID. Replace {order_id} with the actual ID, e.g. orders://ORD-1042.",
            mimeType="application/json",
        ),
    ]

@app.read_resource()
async def read_resource(uri: str):
    match = re.match(r"orders://(.+)", uri)
    if match:
        order_id = match.group(1)
        order = fetch_order_from_db(order_id)   # your DB query
        if order is None:
            return [TextContent(type="text", text=f'{{"error": "Order {order_id} not found"}}')]
        import json
        return [TextContent(type="text", text=json.dumps(order, default=str))]
    return [TextContent(type="text", text=f"Unknown resource URI: {uri}")]
Tools are the action side of MCP. Each tool has a
handler receives the tool name and parsed arguments and returns a list of
Read-Only Tool (safe to call without confirmation)
Write Tool (requires user confirmation in host)
— saved message templates that clients can surface to users as slash commands or quick actions. Prompts can include arguments that are filled in at invocation time.
No — parameterised query with side-effect-free result
Yes — if the set is finite; use URI template
Yes — tool; or inject in system prompt
Yes — list all, read by URI

MCP supports optional resource subscriptions. When a client subscribes to a resource URI, the server can notify it whenever the resource changes. The client re-reads the resource on notification rather than polling.

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.types import Resource, TextContent
import asyncio

app = Server("live-config-server")

# Track active subscriptions
_subscribers: set[str] = set()

@app.subscribe_resource()
async def subscribe(uri: str) -> None:
    _subscribers.add(uri)

@app.unsubscribe_resource()
async def unsubscribe(uri: str) -> None:
    _subscribers.discard(uri)

async def notify_config_changed():
    """Call this whenever the config file changes on disk."""
    uri = "config://app-settings"
    if uri in _subscribers:
        # Notify the client — it will re-read via read_resource()
        await app.request_context.session.send_resource_updated(uri)

# Trigger notification from an external watcher
async def watch_config(path: str):
    import hashlib
    last_hash = None
    while True:
        with open(path, "rb") as f:
            content = f.read()
        current_hash = hashlib.sha256(content).hexdigest()
        if last_hash is not None and current_hash != last_hash:
            await notify_config_changed()
        last_hash = current_hash
        await asyncio.sleep(5)   # poll every 5 seconds
Subscriptions are optional — clients that do not support them simply call
when they need fresh data. Servers that do not support subscriptions omit the
handler and the client falls back to polling.
Combining Resources and Tools in One Server

Most real servers expose both resources and tools. A good pattern: expose the data model as resources and the operations on that data as tools. This gives the LLM read access to browse the data and write access to act on it, while the access-control boundary stays clear.
to see what is there, then call
to modify it. Resources give the LLM eyes; tools give it hands.
Resources and tools serve different mental models.

Resources are data the LLM reads, like opening a file. Tools are operations the LLM performs, like calling an API. When in doubt: if the client should decide when to fetch it, it is a resource; if the LLM should decide when to invoke it, it is a tool.
